coredata.dc:title: One-step hydrothermal approach to fabricate carbon dots from apple juice for imaging of mycobacterium and fungal cells
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Mehta, Vaibhavkumar N.
Jha, Sanjay
Basu, Hirakendu
Singhal, Rakesh Kumar
Kailasa, Suresh Kumar
coredata.dc:description: A facile green hydrothermal method was developed for the preparation of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) using apple juice as a raw material. The synthesized fluorescent CDs were characterized by UV–vis, fluorescence, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), dynamic light scattering (DLS), high resolution transmission electron microscopic (HR-TEM), life-time measurement and laser scanning confocal microscopic techniques. The CDs showed bright blue emission under UV-light (λ ex =365nm). The CDs were used as alternative biocompatible fluorescent probes for imaging of bacteria (Mycobacterium tuberculosis and Pseudomonas aeruginosa) and fungal (Magnaporthe oryzae). It was shown that the prepared CDs had no toxic effect on the both cells lines (bacteria and fungal), indicating that the CDs did not inhibit growth of bacterial and fungal cells, which confirms that the CDs exhibit good biocompatibility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Apple juice
Laser scanning confocal microscopy
HR-TEM
Bacteria and fungal cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots from apple juice
Confocal microscopic imaging of cells using CDs as probes
Minimal inhibitor concentration (MIC) evaluation of CDs toward M. tuberculosis H37Rv, P. aeruginosa and M. oryzae
Cytotoxicity assay of CDs for P. aeruginosa and M. oryzae
Calculation for the quantum yield of CDs
The CDs as probes for imaging of bacteria (M. tuberculosis and P. aeruginosa) and fungal (M. oryzae) cells
Evaluation of MIC of CDs toward M. tuberculosis H37Rv, P. aeruginosa and M. oryzae
Cytotoxicity of CDs on P. aeruginosa and M. oryzae

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent nature of CDs under UV light illumination at different excitation wavelengths (254, 302 and 365nm) and its application as a fluorescent probe for the imaging of M. tuberculosis.
(a) FT-IR spectrum and (b) XRD pattern of CDs.
HR-TEM images of CDs at different magnifications at (a) 20nm, (b) 5nm, (c) lattice spacing in interfringes in CDs, (d) particle size distribution histogram of CDs and (e) DLS of CDs.
UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation (λ ex =368nm) and emission (λ em =475nm) spectra of CDs. Inset picture shows the CDs under daylight (left) and UV light illumination at 365nm (right).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs obtained at different excitation wavelengths progressively increasing from 300nm to 540nm with a 20nm increment; (b) the fluorescence decay (life time) curve of the CDs upon excitation at 368nm.

Confocal laser microscopic images of (a) M. tuberculosis; (b) P. aeruginosa and (c) M. oryzae after incubation at 37°C for 1–6h (i) bright field without CDs, fluorescence mode images with CDs at excitation wavelengths, (ii) 405 (blue), (iii) 488 (green), and (iv) 561 (red) nm. The concentration of the CDs is 10μg/mL (For interpretation of the references to color in figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of the article.)
Cytotoxicity of CDs on (a) P. aeruginosa and (b) M. oryzae (10, 40, 100, 200, 400mg/mL). The values are plotted as mean±SD (n =3).
Comparison of optical characteristics and applications of the CDs derived from apple juice with the reported methods.

row.entry: Human breast cancer MCF-7 cells imaging
Colorimetric detection of H2O2 and glucose
Colorimetric detection of Al3+ ions and imaging of 786–0 cells
M. tuberculosis, P. aeruginosa and M. oryzae imaging

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This work was orally presented at 1st International Caparica Conference on Chromogenic and Emissive Materials, 8th–10th September 2014, Lisbon, Portugal.

head.ce:title: One-step hydrothermal approach to fabricate carbon dots from apple juice for imaging of mycobacterium and fungal cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Applied Chemistry Department, S. V. National Institute of Technology, Surat 395 007, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Applied Chemistry Department, S. V. National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Gujarat Agricultural Biotechnology Institute, Navsari Agricultural University, Surat 395007, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Gujarat Agricultural Biotechnology Institute, Navsari Agricultural University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical Chemistry Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Center, Trombay, Mumbai 400085, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Analytical Chemistry Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Center

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile green hydrothermal method was developed for the preparation of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) using apple juice as a raw material. The synthesized fluorescent CDs were characterized by UV–vis, fluorescence, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), dynamic light scattering (DLS), high resolution transmission electron microscopic (HR-TEM), life-time measurement and laser scanning confocal microscopic techniques. The CDs showed bright blue emission under UV-light (λ ex =365nm). The CDs were used as alternative biocompatible fluorescent probes for imaging of bacteria (Mycobacterium tuberculosis and Pseudomonas aeruginosa) and fungal (Magnaporthe oryzae). It was shown that the prepared CDs had no toxic effect on the both cells lines (bacteria and fungal), indicating that the CDs did not inhibit growth of bacterial and fungal cells, which confirms that the CDs exhibit good biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:para: In the ever-expanding field of nanomaterial research, the CDs have attracted much interest over the last decade due to their remarkable novel properties such as biocompatibility, low toxicity, easy functionalization, chemical inertness, photostability, and valuable photoluminescence [1,2]. In recent years, the CDs have proved to be as potential eco-friendly fluorescent probes for visualizing structural or functional images of living systems down to the molecular level. The advent of new optical properties of CDs has made the research on the development of various “smart” nanosystems such as bioimaging [3–6], biochemical assays [7,8], drug delivery [9], photocatalysis [10] and light-emitting devices [11,12]. As a result, much efforts have been focused on the development of various synthetic approaches for the preparation of fluorescent CDs including laser ablation [13,14], arc discharge [15], pyrolysis [16], oxidation [17], electrochemical exfoliation [18], hydrothermal treatment [19], plasma treatment [20], microwave irradiation [21–24] and microwave/ultrasonic passivation [25,26]. On the other hand, even though the above methods were successfully applied to prepare CDs, unfortunately they require tedious process, sophisticated instrumental set-up, limited spectral efficiency, and low product yield. In view of green chemistry approach, there is an urgent need to establish a new green synthetic approach for the preparation of fluorescent CDs and their applications in bioimaging.

Over the past few years, hydrothermal carbonization has proved as an eco-friendly, traditional and soft chemical route for the preparation of CDs in aqueous media, which produces highly efficient fluorescent probes for recognizing various chemical species and cells in vitro and in vivo [27]. Hydrothermal synthesis of CDs using various renewal carbon sources has provided great advancement over existing physical methods (laser ablation, arc discharge and plasma treatment), which is due to its simplicity and production of CDs with good quantum yield. In hydrothermal method, CDs were prepared by using either low-cost or biowaste materials as raw materials. For example, CDs were successfully prepared by using various renewable resources as precursors including chitosan [28], coffee grounds [29], watermelon peel [30], pomelo peel [31], Trapa bispinosa peel [32], orange juice [33], gelatin [34], and low-cost organic chemicals [35,36]. It can be noticed that the CDs were successfully generated using bio-waste/plant materials as raw materials, which avoids the use of costly/toxic chemicals or large amounts of chemicals and complicated post-treatment processes.

Furthermore, Li's and Zhang's groups [37] developed a new strategy for the preparation of two types of CDs with either excitation-independent blue emission or distinctive excitation-dependent full-color emissions using chloroform and diethylamine as precursors. Tian and co-workers [38] developed a CDs-based fluorescence detection method for sensing of reactive oxygen species in biological tissues. A novel and large-scale strategy was developed for the preparation of N-doped CDs with high yield by pyrolyzing ethanolamine as a precursor [39]. Dong et al. [40] described one-step microwave method for large-scale fabrication of water dispersible fluorescent CDs using citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors for bioimaging of cells and sensing of Hg2+ ion. Shao's group [41] prepared N-doped graphite-like phase CDs for multicolor real-time imaging of cancer cells. Wang's and Chen's teams [42] reported a simple green synthetic method for the fabrication of fluorescent CDs using hair as a raw material. Bendicho et al. [43] developed in situ ultrasound-assisted synthetic method for preparation of CDs using fructose as a carbon source for the detection of methylmercury. Recently, various natural precursors such as konjac flour [44], bamboo leaves [45], sugar cane juice [46] and milk [47] have been used as carbon sources for the preparation of fluorescent CDs and acted as probes for imaging of cells and sensing of inorganic species. Furthermore, Jun et al. [48] illustrated the use of apple juice as a carbon source for the preparation of fluorescent CDs and used as probes for sensing of Hg2+ ion in environmental water samples. Abdollahi's group [49] also synthesized CDs from fruit juice and used as a fluorescent probe for the sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ ion with a detection limit of 14nM. These reports illustrated that the selection of precursors play a key role to modify the surface chemistry of CDs with multiple functional groups for sensing and cellular imaging of target analytes in biocomplex samples. To date, no reports are available for imaging of bacteria (Mycobacterium tuberculosis, Pseudomonas aeruginosa) and fungal (Magnaporthe oryzae) cells using apple juice derived CDs as imaging probes.

Herein, we report a simple hydrothermal method for the preparation of fluorescent CDs using apple juice as a raw material. The prepared CDs are highly water-soluble, nano-sized (4.5±1.0nm) and emitted bright blue fluorescence. The surface chemistry, size, and morphology of CDs were characterized by FT-IR, DLS and HR-TEM. The multicolor CDs are quite stable against photo-bleaching as compared with organic dyes and used as fluorescent probes for imaging of bacteria (P. aeruginosa and M. tuberculosis) and fungal (M. oryzae) cells (Scheme 1 ).

Malus domestica (apple) was purchased (Rupees 50/- for 500g) from the local market of Surat, India and used as a precursor. Dichloromethane, sodium chloride, glucose, glycerol and dimethyl sulfoxide were obtained from Merck Ltd., India. All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Milli-Q-purified water was used for sample preparations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots from apple juice
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent CDs were synthesized via hydrothermal carbonization method using apple juice as a carbon source [48]. Briefly, 500g of apples was chopped into small pieces and converted into liquid by adding 500mL of water. Vacuum filtration was carried out in order to obtain the pulp free juice for the carbonization. The resulting mixture was transferred into a 700mL stainless-steel autoclave, heated at constant temperature of 150°C for 12h, and then allowed to cool down naturally. The obtained dark brown solution was filtered through 0.45μm filter to remove large particles and then washed with dichloromethane to remove unreacted organic moieties. The aqueous solution was collected and centrifuged at 15,000rpm for 20min to remove solid residues and further dialyzed for 24h against double distilled water. The obtained CDs were stored at 4°C for further characterization and cell labeling.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confocal microscopic imaging of cells using CDs as probes
ce:section.ce:para: P. aeruginosa colonies were grown on a Luria-Bertani (LB) medium (10mg/mL Tryptone, 5mg/mL Yeast Extract and 10mg/mL NaCl pH 7.0) at 37°C, 180rpm. The colonies were used to inoculate with 10mL of fresh LB medium and grown overnight at 37°C. The Lowenstein and Jensen (L.J.) medium was harvested in 0.85% saline in bijou bottles and used as a medium for the culture of M. tuberculosis H37Rv. The above culture tubes were incubated at 37±1°C for 24h followed by streaking of M. tuberculosis H37Rv (5×104 bacilli per tube). The growth of bacteria was seen after 12, 22 and 28 days of incubation. M. oryzae was cultured on complete medium of 5×YEG (0.5% yeast extract and 2% glucose) and oatmeal agar (OA) Plates 25°C [50]. To enhance the culture growth, plates were grown under constant fluorescence light at room temperature. The cultures were vortexed at 150rpm, and their growth was monitored by measuring the absorbance at 600nm. Bacteria (P. aeruginosa and M. tuberculosis) and fungal (M. Oryzae) cells were collected from the middle of exponential growth phase and used for confocal microscopic studies. Briefly, 70% (v/v) ethanol was used for the internalization of CDs by both bacterial and fungal cells at 4°C for 5min. The staining of cells were carried out by re-suspending of cells in 100mM of phosphate buffer that contained 10μg/mL of CDs. The cells-CDs-conjugates were washed thrice with double distilled water and their images were measured by using a Carl Zeiss 510 LSM laser scanning confocal microscope in blue, green, and red regions with laser excitations at 405, 488, and 561nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Minimal inhibitor concentration (MIC) evaluation of CDs toward M. tuberculosis H37Rv, P. aeruginosa and M. oryzae
ce:section.ce:para: The MIC of CDs toward M. tuberculosis H37Rv was performed using L. J. method [51,52]. The stock solutions of primary (500, 250 and 125μg/mL) and secondary (100, 50, 25, 10 and 1.0μg/mL) dilutions of CDs in dimethyl sulfoxide were added into the liquid L. J. medium and then medium was sterilized by inspissations method. The bacterial growth was observed after 12, 22 and 28 days of incubation. The test tubes (target bacteria with CDs) were compared with the control tubes where medium alone was incubated with M. tuberculosis H37Rv. The concentration at which no development of colonies occurred or less than 20 colonies was taken as the MIC concentration of CDs.

The MIC of synthesized CDs was evaluated by agar streak dilution method against bacterial (P. aeruginosa) and fungal (M. Oryzae) cells. Briefly, the stock solutions of CDs (10, 50, 100, 250 and 500μg/mL) were added in a molten sterile agar, i.e. nutrient agar for the growth of bacterial (P. aeruginosa) cells and sabouraud dextrose agar for the growth of fungal (M. Oryzae) cells, respectively. The media containing the different concentrations of CDs were subjected to the petri dish at a depth of 4–5mm and allowed to solidify the media under sterilized conditions. The suspensions of P. aeruginosa and M. Oryzae (105 CFU/mL) were applied to the above media and incubated for 24h (P. aeruginosa) and for 48h (M. Oryzae) at 37±1°C. The MIC of CDs was estimated by the development of visible growth in agar media and their photographs were shown in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity assay of CDs for P. aeruginosa and M. oryzae
ce:section.ce:para:
The cytotoxicity of CDs toward P. aeruginosa was carried out in modified M9 medium in 150mL Erlenmeyer flasks for 12–16h at 37°C. Different concentrations of CDs (0, 10, 40, 100, 200 and 400mg/mL) were added into the above flasks and vortexed for 2min at 180rpm. Furthermore, 2mL of culture broth was collected at different time intervals (0–30h) and measured their optical density at 600nm in order to calculate the cell viability. Results are quantified as relative values to that of the negative control, where the negative control (untreated) is set to 100% viability. The cytotoxicity of CDs was estimated on M. oryzae by calculating % spore germination. Briefly, M. oryzae spores (105 spores/mL) were germinated in sterile yeast extract glucose broth containing various concentration of CDs (0, 10, 40, 100, 200 and 400mg/mL) for 24h at 25°C. The percentage of spore germination in the presence of CDs was estimated by comparing 100% of germination in medium without CDs. The following equation was used to calculate % spore germination.
% spore germination =
Spore
germination
in
presence
of
CDs × 100
Spore
germination
in
medium
with
buffer ( control )

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation for the quantum yield of CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence quantum yield of CDs was determined by using quinine sulfate (% QY is 54 in 0.1M H2SO4 solution) as a standard sample and calculated by following equation [53].
Q
CDs
=
Q R
⋅
I
CDs
I R
⋅
A R
A
CDs
⋅
η
CDs
2
η R 2
where, Q represents the quantum yield, I represents the fluorescence intensity, A refers the absorbance measured at excited wavelength, and η represents the refractive index of the solvent used. The subscript “R” denotes the reference sample and CDs denotes the carbon dots. The quantum yield was found to be 4.27% at excitation wavelength 360nm.

UV–vis spectra were measured by using Maya Pro 2000 spectrophotometer (Ocean Optics, USA). The fluorescence analysis was carried out by using a RF-5301 PC Shimadzu spectroflourometer. FT-IR spectra were recorded on a Perkin Elmer (FT-IR spectrum BX, Germany). Fluorescence lifetime was measured with Horiba Jobin Yvon IBH Fluorocube instruments after exciting 370nm picosecond diode laser. The morphology and microstructure of CDs were examined by HR-TEM on a JEOL 3010 with an accelerating voltage of 200kV. The samples for HR-TEM were made by dropping an aqueous solution onto a 300-mesh copper grid coated with a lacy carbon film. XRD pattern of CDs were recorded on a Rigaku diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) equipped with graphite monochromatized CuKα (λ =0.15405nm) radiation in the range from 10° to 70°. DLS measurements were performed by using Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, UK). Fluorescence microscopic cell-imaging was carried out by using Carl Zeiss 510 LSM laser scanning confocal microscope.

The CDs were successfully synthesized by the hydrothermal route using apple juice as a raw material since apple contains a wide variety of organic molecules (carbohydrates, polyphenols and volatile organic compounds). To convert these organic molecules into carbon nanostructures, hydrothermal carbonization was carried out at 150°C for 12h. As shown in Scheme 1, the CDs show strong blue emission under ultraviolet illumination at 302 and 365nm, confirming that the formation of fluorescent CDs. The synthesized CDs were confirmed by FT-IR, XRD, DLS and HR-TEM. Fig. 1a shows the FT-IR spectrum of CDs. A broad absorption band at 3200–3500cm−1 is originated from OH stretching vibrations of CDs. The characteristic absorption bands at 2888, 1765, 1654, 1552 and 1177cm−1 correspond to stretching vibrations of CH, CO, CC, CN, and CO groups, respectively. The peaks at 1419 and 1067cm−1 are assigned to asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of COC group and the peak at around 1400cm−1 is assigned to stretching vibration of unsaturated carbon atoms (sp 2 hybridization), indicating that the prepared CDs contain olefinic configuration. The presence of epoxy group in FT-IR spectrum was proved from the bands at 2996 and 937cm−1 correspond to stretching vibrations of νCH and γCO groups in CDs. The XRD pattern of CDs shows broader peak at about 2θ =23.4°–24.6°, revealing an amorphous carbon phase in the CDs. The sharp peaks at 28.20°, 40.40°, 50.10° and 58.56° are corresponded to the diffractions of (002), (101), (102) and (103) planes, indicating that the presence of turbostratic and graphitic carbon in the CDs (Fig. 1b). Fig. 2a–d shows the HR-TEM images of CDs with the particle size distribution, indicating that the CDs are well dispersed in solution with spherical shape. Fig. 2d shows the lattice structure of CDs with an interfringes distance of 0.32nm, indicating that the presence of sp 2 graphitic carbon (002) plane, which is in good agreement with the XRD peak obtained at 28.20°. The particle size distribution of CDs was calculated by measuring several hundred particles, revealing that the CDs have an average size of 4.5±1.0nm (Fig. 2d). Similarly, we also studied the DLS for estimation of hydrodynamic diameter of CDs (Fig. 2e). It was found that the hydrodynamic diameter of CDs is mainly distributed in the range of 1.5nm–6.5nm with an average hydrodynamic diameter of ∼3.72nm, which is in good accordance with HR-TEM data (Fig. 2a–d).

The UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra of CDs were shown in Fig. 3 . In UV–vis spectrum, the CDs exhibit two absorption peaks at 250 and 300nm, confirming the n–π* and π–π* transitions, which indicates that the presence of CO and CC groups in CDs [5,20,28]. It can be noticed that the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of CDs shows a strong emission peak centered at 475nm when excited at 368nm, indicating that the prepared CDs are in fluorescent nature. The inset shows photographs of CDs under visible (left: daylight) and under UV illumination at 365nm (right). As shown in Fig. 3, the PL spectrum of CDs exhibits a maximum emission peak at 475nm when excited at 368nm which is quite higher than the emission peak obtained when excited at 300nm (Fig. 4a, black line). As a large number of particles being excited at 368nm wavelength gives rise to maximum emission intensity at around 475nm, resulting a bright blue fluorescence of CDs. This was attributed to the surface defects, zigzag sites and band gap like transition of different functional groups present on the surface of CDs [13,54,55]. The full width at half maximum is about 94.21nm, suggesting that the relatively small size distribution of CDs, which was consistent with HR-TEM data. It is well known that the transition modes of surface are known to have different probabilities at different excitation wavelengths due to the presence of various functional groups on the surface of CDs. Each transition plays a dominating role at its corresponding excitation wavelength, which results a shift in the second region of emission spectra.

In order to confirm excitation-dependent emission characteristics of CDs, we studied the PL emission spectra of CDs using progressively increased excitation wavelengths from 300 to 540nm (Fig. 4a). It can be observed that a red-shift was attributed in the emission spectra of CDs from 465nm to 565nm with increasing excitation wavelengths, accompanied with the decrease of the fluorescence intensity, revealing that the fluorescence of CDs is strongly dependent on the excitation wavelength. However, the red shift in the emission spectra was attributed to the variation in the size of CDs as well as the presence of different energy levels associated with different “surface states” formed by various functional groups (CO, CO and OCOH) that become emissive upon stabilization as a result of the surface passivation [33,35]. Moreover, the CDs were found to possess clear upconversion PL properties upon excitation, the PL spectra show varying emission peaks between 360nm to 540nm, which are different from the previous reports [16,25]. It is well known that the energy difference between the excitation and emission in the upconversion process is not a fixed value, the upconversion mechanism of CDs is easily attributed to the multiphoton active process due to anti-Stokes photoluminescence. The prepared CDs exhibit a maximum PL quantum yield of up to 4.27% (excited at 368nm), which is comparable with the quantum yield of previously reported method for the preparation of CDs using apple juice as a precursor [48]. In order to measure the life time of CDs, we also studied the PL decay curve of CDs at 475nm (blue emission) with an excitation at 368nm. The lifetime data of CDs is very well fitted to a triple-exponential function (Fig. 4b). The lifetimes of CDs are found to be τ 1 =3.67ns, τ 2 =0.83ns, τ 3 =11.32ns and the calculated average lifetime of CDs is 5.27ns. In order to confirm the reproducibility of the method with regard to fluorescence properties of CDs, we studied the reproducibility of PL emission spectra of CDs by synthesizing CDs several times (intra-day). Supporting Information of Fig. S1a shows the PL emission spectra of CDs (λ ex =368nm) at five different experiments. It was observed that the PL emission peak positions were not shifted more than 5–10nm with the relative standard deviation (%RSD) value of 2.97%. Moreover, the CDs exhibit bright blue emission under UV light illuminations at 365 and 302nm, respectively (Supporting Information of Fig. S1b). These results demonstrate that the present method shows good reproducibility for the synthesis of CDs. It is well known that the photostability of CDs plays a key role in bioimaging of cells. In this connection, we studied the emission behavior of the CDs under continuous UV light illumination at 365nm for 120min. It was also noticed that the photobleaching of CDs is not observed and the fluorescence intensity of CDs is remained constant even after 120min of continuous UV light illumination, indicating the good photostability of CDs (Supporting Information of Fig. S2). Table 1 shows the comparison of the optical properties and applications of the CDs derived from apple juice with the reported methods [13–26]. It can be seen that the apple juice derived CDs provide relatively good reproducibility, photostability and narrow particle size distribution with the comparable quantum yield over the other methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The CDs as probes for imaging of bacteria (M. tuberculosis and P. aeruginosa) and fungal (M. oryzae) cells
ce:section.ce:para: M. tuberculosis infection causes tuberculosis and remains a major health concern in the developing world and among HIV-positive people. It is very important to develop effective vaccines by studying the interactions of M. tuberculosis peptides with drug molecules. Due to their excellent optical properties and good biocompatibility nature, the CDs were further explored as fluorescent probes for imaging of bacteria (M. tuberculosis and P. aeruginosa) and fungal (M. oryzae) cells. Fig. 5a(ii)–(iv) shows the confocal microscopic images of M. tuberculosis after incubation with CDs. It can be observed that the strong blue, green and red fluorescence from CDs-labeled cells depending on the excitation wavelengths at 405nm (blue), 488nm (green) and 561nm (red), whereas no fluorescence was observed from the control sample without CDs. In order to explore these CDs as probes for multiple cell imaging, the CDs were introduced into bacterial (P. aeruginosa) and fungal (M. oryzae) cells for in vitro bioimaging and the images were recorded using confocal microscopy. Compared with the control cells of bacterial (P. aeruginosa) and fungal (M. oryzae) in the absence of CDs (Fig. 5b(i) and c(i)), bacterial and fungal cells incubated with CDs became brighter owing to the strong fluorescence emitting from CDs in cytosol, showing blue, green and red colors upon excitation at 405nm, 488nm and 561nm, respectively (Fig. 5b and c(ii)–(iv)). These results revealed that the large amount of CDs was internalized into cells by entrapping endosomes, multivesicular bodies, and lysosomes via endocytosis [56]. Since, the prepared CDs are hydrophilic in nature with active functional groups (carbonyl, hydroxy and carboxylic acid), resulting an effective internalization of CDs into the cytosol and nuclear compartments with a broad distribution of fluorescent labeled CDs, which indicates that the prepared CDs can be efficiently internalized into the cells and to be used as nanocarriers for drug delivery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of MIC of CDs toward M. tuberculosis H37Rv, P. aeruginosa and M. oryzae
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the potential application of CDs as bioimaging probes, M. tuberculosis H37Rv, P. aeruginosa and M. oryzae were subjected to the different concentrations of CDs (1.0, 10, 25, 50, 100, 250 and 500μg/mL) to evaluate MIC. Supporting Information of Fig. 3a shows the changes in bacterial concentration resulting from exposure to the different concentration of CDs. It can be noticed that the CDs did not dramatically inhibit growth of M. tuberculosis H37Rv strain even at 500μg/mL of CDs. Furthermore, it was observed that the 500μg/mL of CDs concentration did not induce the death of M. tuberculosis bacteria whereas only 10μg/mL concentration of CDs is required for the imaging of M. tuberculosis. Therefore, the CDs can be used as the potent candidates for the in vivo cellular imaging of M. tuberculosis. Supporting Information of Fig. 3b and c shows the photographic images of zone of inhibition study of CDs toward bacterial (P. aeruginosa) and fungal (M. oryzae) strains. These results demonstrate that the no inhibitory zone was observed in case of both species (P. aeruginosa and M. oryzae) even at 500μg/mL of CDs, indicating that the biocompatible nature of CDs toward bacterial and fungal cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity of CDs on P. aeruginosa and M. oryzae
ce:section.ce:para: Cytotoxicity of CDs toward bacterial (P. aeruginosa) and fungal (M. oryzae) strains were evaluated to use them as potential probes for bioimaging. Fig. 6a shows cell viability of P. aeruginosa after incubation with CDs in the concentration range from 10 to 400mg/mL for 30h. These results indicated that the cytotoxicity of CDs was negligible up to 100mg/mL. Then, the cell viability gradually decreased with increasing concentration of CDs from 100 to 200mg/mL. Furthermore, we also studied the germination of M. oryzae spores (105 spores/mL) in sterile yeast extract glucose broth containing various concentration of CDs (0, 10, 40, 100, 200 and 400mg/mL) for 24h at 25°C. As shown in Fig. 6b, the CDs had no effect on the seedling growth of M. oryzae, confirming that the CDs show negligible effect on the germination of M. oryzae. It can be observed that the percentage of germination is constant (99%) at CDs concentrations ranging from 10 to 400mg/mL, which indicates that the CDs can be used as biocompatible nanocomposite on the germination of fungus. These results suggest that the CDs did not show any significant effects on wetland plant communities. To support this result, recently Shen's and Cui's groups [57] studied the toxicity of CDs on bacteria, rats and mice under various doses. It was noticed that there is no abnormality or lesion in the major organs of rats treated with the CDs and the CDs did not exhibit any gene toxicity. Similarly, toxicity of three nanoparticles (CdTe QDs, Au NPs and CDs) was performed on a variety of cell lines (HeLa, MCF-7, NIH/3T3 cells) as well as live plants (green gram sprouts) [58]. These parallel comparative studies clearly demonstrated that the relative toxicity of the nanomaterials is bare CdTe QDs>Au NPs>CDs, and their IC50 values for normal NIH/3T3 cells were found to be 0.98μg/mL, 62μg/mL, and >250μg/mL, respectively. These reports clearly indicate that the higher doses of CDs (<250μg/mL) did not exert any significant toxic effect on the cells. Therefore, the survival rates are found to be 99% in the CDs-treated culture medium, without significant cell death, indicating that the CDs can be used as potential candidates for in vitro and in vivo studies. Thus, the as-prepared CDS have good biocompatibility and potential use in vivo imaging, biolabeling, and drug delivery.

In the present work, water dispersible fluorescent CDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal carbonization using apple juice as a precursor without further chemical modification. The surfaces of CDs were functionalized with multifunctional groups (hydroxy, keto, carboxylic acid, and amino) and showed good QY (4.27%). Moreover, the spectroscopic (UV–vis, fluorescence, FT-IR, and DLS) and microscopic (HR-TEM and XRD) results indicated that the CDs have small oxygenous turbostratic and graphitic carbons domains, which endow them with fluorescent properties. The CDs successfully acted as probes for in vitro imaging of bacteria (M. tuberculosis and P. aeruginosa) and fungal (M. oryzae) cells. The CDs were effectively internalized into the cells and showed strong fluorescence of CDs within cells. The MIC and cytotoxicity assays revealed that the CDs are highly biocompatible. These results imply the potential value of CDs for a new type fluorescence marker, bio-sensors, biomedical imaging, and drug delivery in nanobiotechnology.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors thank Prof. Z. V. P. Murthy, Chemical Engineering Department, S. V. National Institute of Technology, Surat for providing DLS instrument facility. Authors also thank Department of Science and Technology, Government of India for providing Maya Pro 2000 spectrophotometer (Ocean Optics, USA) under the Fast-Track Young Scientist Scheme (Ref. No: SR/FT/CS-54/2010). Mr. V. N. Mehta greatly acknowledge Department of Science and Technology, Government of India (Ref. No: SB/ITS-Y/02901/2014-15) for giving financial support to present this work at 1st International Caparica Conference on Chromogenic and Emissive Materials, 8th–10th September 2014, Lisbon, Portugal.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2015.02.104.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles: synthesis, characterization, and bioimaging application
Carbon dots for optical imaging in vivo
Preparation of multicolor emitting carbon dots for HeLa cell imaging
Development of multicolor carbon nanoparticles for cell imaging
Hg (II) sensing based on functionalized carbon dots obtained by direct laser ablation
Highly selective detection of phosphate in very complicated matrixes with an off-on fluorescent probe of europium-adjusted carbon dots
Hollow luminescent carbon dots for drug delivery
Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design
White light-emitting devices based on carbon dots’ electroluminescence
Deep ultraviolet photoluminescence of water-soluble self-passivated graphene quantum dots
Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence

One-pot green synthesis of optically pH sensitive carbon dots with up conversion luminescence
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles derived from candle soot

Carbon dots prepared from ginger exhibiting efficient inhibition of human hepatocellular carcinoma cells

Water-soluble and phosphorus-containing carbon dots with strong green fluorescence for cell labeling
Naked oats derived dual-emission carbon nanodots for ratiometric sensing and cellular imaging
Microwave-assisted polyol synthesis of gadolinium-doped green luminescent carbon dots as a bimodal nanoprobe
One-step ultrasonic synthesis of fluorescent N-doped carbon dots from glucose and their visible-light sensitive photocatalytic ability
One-step ultrasonic synthesis of water-soluble carbon nanoparticles with excellent photoluminescent properties
Nanoscale optical probes for cellular imaging
One-step synthesis of amino-functionalized fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by hydrothermal carbonization of chitosan
Synthesis and analytical applications of photoluminescent carbon nanodots
Facile synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots using watermelon peel as a carbon source
Economical, green synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their use as probes for sensitive and selective detection of Mercury (II) ions
Green synthesis of biocompatible carbon dots using aqueous extract of Trapa bispinosa peel
Simple one-step synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots from orange juice: application as excellent bio-imaging agents
Easy synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots from gelatin and their luminescent properties and applications
Preparation of carbon quantum dots with tunable photoluminescence by rapid laser passivation in ordinary organic solvents
Synthesis of high-quality carbon nanodots from hydrophilic compounds: role of functional groups
Carbon dots with continuously tunable full-color emission and their application in ratiometric pH sensing
Carbon-dot-based ratiometric fluorescent probe for imaging and biosensing of superoxide anion in live cells
Fast one-step synthesis of N-doped carbon dots by pyrolyzing ethanolamine
Multifunctional water-soluble luminescent carbon dots for imaging and Hg2+ sensing
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots with heterogeneous multi-layered structures
Hair-derived carbon dots toward versatile multidimensional fluorescent materials
In situ building of a nanoprobe based on fluorescent carbon dots for methylmercury detection
Green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots from konjac flour with off–on fluorescence by Fe3+ and l-lysine for bioimaging
One-step green synthesized fluorescent carbon nanodots from bamboo leaves for copper (II) ion detection
One-pot green synthesis of carbon dots by using Saccharum officinarum juice for fluorescent imaging of bacteria (Escherichia coli) and yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) cells
Green synthesis of luminescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots from milk and its imaging application
Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon quantum dots for detection of Hg2+
Synthesis of biocompatible and highly photoluminescent nitrogen doped carbon dots from lime: analytical applications and optimization using response surface methodology
Two novel fungal virulence genes specifically expressed in appressoria of the rice blast fungus

sb:publisher.sb:name: ASM Press, American Society for Microbiology

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advances in techniques of testing mycobacterial drug sensitivity, and the use of sensitivity tests in tuberculosis control programmes
A green and facile approach for the synthesis of water soluble fluorescent carbon dots from banana juice
Synthesis of highly luminescent graphitized carbon dots and the application in the Hg2+ detection
Biodistribution study of carbogenic dots in cells and in vivo for optical imaging
Systematic safety evaluation on photoluminescent carbon dots
Parallel comparative studies on the toxic effects of unmodified CdTe quantum dots, gold nanoparticles, and carbon nanodots on live cells as well as green gram sprouts

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Vaibhavkumar N. Mehta completed his Master degree in Nanoscience and Nanotechnology from Sardar Patel University, Gujarat. Currently he is doing his Ph.D. at S. V. National Institute of Technology, Surat. His research interest is functionalization of nanoparticles for colorimetric sensing of biomolecules and metal ions.

Sanjay Jha completed his Ph.D in Microbiology from M.S. University, Baroda, Gujarat. Currently, he is working as Associate Professor, Gujarat Agricultural Biotechnology Institute, Navsari Agricultural University, Surat, India. He is interested in nanomaterials interactions with cells and their confirmations by microscopic tools.

Hirakendu Basu is working as Scientist, Analytical Chemistry Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Center, India. His research area is on the development of analytical methods for the analysis of trace metal ions in different environmental matrices.

Rakesh Kumar Singhal passed BARC Training School (32nd batch) from Chemistry stream. He has obtained his master degree in chemistry from I.I.T. Delhi and Ph.D. (Chemistry) from Mumbai University in 1996. He is the Head, Analytical Spectroscopy Section of Analytical Chemistry Division of Bhabha Atomic Research Center, India is responsible for development of analytical spectroscopic techniques for the measurement of traces of various elements in different environmental matrices and samples originating from different chemical processes. He has authored more than 150 publications in International & national Journals, Symposium and Book Chapters, in the field of environmental & radioanalytical chemistry.

Suresh Kumar Kailasa completed his Ph.D. in Chemistry from S. V. University, Tirupati in 2005. After completing his two postdoctoral fellowships at South Korea and at National Sun Yat-Sen University, Taiwan he joined as a faculty at S. V. National Institute of Technology, Surat in 2009. He received Young Scientist Award from Taiwan Mass Spectrometry Society in 2013. His interest includes nanomaterials integration in MALDI-MS and development of nanomaterials-based colorimetric sensors for biomolecules, drugs and metal ions.
